advantages: Uses the exact full-batch gradient—no sampling variance, so loss decreases monotonically on convex problems with appropriate η.
Conceptually simple: one update rule θ ← θ − η∇L(θ) underlies all gradient-based optimizers including SGD and Adam.
Stable per-epoch loss curves make debugging sign errors, learning rate and implementation bugs easier than noisy mini-batch training.
Deterministic given fixed data and initialization—reproducible runs without batch-order randomness.
disadvantages: Each epoch requires a forward and backward pass over the entire dataset—prohibitively slow and memory-heavy for large-scale Gen AI training.
One update per epoch yields slow progress compared to many mini-batch steps on the same data per epoch.
Still only a first-order local method: no guarantee of reaching a global minimum on non-convex deep learning loss surfaces.
Sensitive to learning rate and conditioning; ill-conditioned valleys can make convergence slow even with exact gradients.
